reasoning:
To resolve positively, the ACLED dataset would need to record strictly more than 10 times the baseline value. As the baseline is 1.000 (meaning there have been 0 valid events in the preceding 360 days to yield exactly 1.000 when 1 is added), there must be at least 11 'Explosions/Remote violence' events in the specific 30-day window preceding each resolution date.

ACLED categorizes 'Explosions/Remote violence' as asymmetrical/political violent events (e.g., airstrikes, IEDs, artillery, grenade attacks by armed groups). Accidental factory explosions, fireworks accidents, or the safe removal of unexploded ordnance (UXO) are not included. 

Slovenia is a highly peaceful state with no ongoing armed conflicts, insurgencies, or violent political unrest. A review of news up to the forecast date (Dec 21, 2025) shows absolutely no incidents of political remote violence in the country. 

For the first resolution date (Dec 28, 2025), 23 days of the 30-day window have already elapsed with 0 events occurring. Accruing 11 events in the remaining 7 days would require a localized outbreak of war or an unprecedented wave of terrorism, which borders on the impossible. The later dates (Jan 20, 2026, and Mar 21, 2026) are similarly implausible; any escalation to the point of 11 bombings in a single month would require massive, slow-building societal breakdown or international conflict, neither of which feature in any current geopolitical forecasts for Slovenia. Thus, the probabilities are set to the absolute allowed floor (0.05) and slightly increased over time to reflect the growing, albeit still trivial, uncertainty.
